text: Hello dear viewer, my name is Dr. Vishal Soni and today we would be watching a case of robotic polycystectomy performed for symptomatic gallstone disease.
The surgeon initiates the procedure with a diagnostic laparoscopy, administering indocyanine green (ICG) at the induction phase to enhance visualization of anatomical structures.
The screen displays the vasculature structures illuminated by the concentration of ICG, highlighting the blood vessels within the surgical field.
The DaVinci XI robotic system is shown in Firefly sensitive mode, highlighting anatomical structures with fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon confirms visualization of the anatomical structures using the DaVinci XI system and proceeds with the insertion of robotic instruments into the surgical field.
The instrument choreography that I personally like is a four-port robotic setup.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook cautery in the right hand and a carrier in the left hand, while the fourth robotic arm, equipped with a clip applicator, is repurposed to retract the liver and gallbladder.
The robotic clip applicator is used for retraction of the liver and gallbladder, demonstrating a different technique compared to the usual laparoscopic approach.
With the instruments inserted, the surgeon uses the Firefly mode on the DaVinci XI system to enhance visualization, delineating the anatomical structures of the stomach and duodenum.

The surgeon defines the neck region of the gallbladder, clearly visualizing the common bile duct (CBD), the right hepatic artery, and the cystic artery. The lymph node is also visible, particularly in patients with less fat in the Calot's triangle region.

The surgeon begins dissection with a small incision at the peritoneum's inferior edge, proceeding anteriorly. Progressive dissection is performed on the anterior aspect of the cystic artery and near the cystic node, using robotic instruments.
The surgeon isolates the cystic artery using robotic instruments, preparing to secure it with either a clip or cautery current.
The surgeon continues the dissection in a U-shaped manner to define the lower edge of the gallbladder, focusing on the neck and infundibulum region.
The surgeon utilizes a robotic platform to perform posterior dissection with a thirty degrees up view, enhancing visibility and maneuverability compared to traditional laparoscopic approaches.

The Ruvia sulcus is clearly visualized as the surgeon incises the peritoneum in the lower one-third of the gallbladder. The anterior dissection continues, with the cystic artery being circumferentially dissected and cauterized.

My practice is that if I encounter a non inflamed gallbladder and the cystic artery is typically less than two millimeters, I would not bother to put a clip. I would just simply cauterize it. And so far it has worked good for me.

The surgeon focuses on the gallbladder triangle, performing a complete clearance of fibro-fatty tissue to create a window. The clearance reveals a segment approximately 1.5 centimeters long, allowing for the placement of three clips.

The surgeon uses the robotic platform to clearly define the biliary anatomy, focusing on the lower one-third of the gallbladder. The area is carefully delineated to check for any aberrant ducts, with none found. A small vessel is visible but deemed insignificant and will be addressed later.

The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection of the cystic duct and proceeds to apply clips using a robotic clip applicator. The instrument, initially used for retraction, is repurposed for clipping, with the applicator visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon applies three clips to the cystic duct, with two clips remaining towards the patient and one towards the specimen. The cystic duct is then divided using the cutting current on a monopolar hook cautery, resulting in a clean and precise cut.
The surgeon employs an open book technique combined with a retrograde approach to address the flabby and enlarged gallbladder, manipulating the organ to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon performs an up-to-down and left-to-right dissection of the gallbladder, entering the loose areolar plane that adheres the gallbladder to the liver. The dissection proceeds smoothly due to the absence of acute cholecystitis, continuing down to the previously clipped area.
The surgeon divides the final tissue attachment between the gallbladder and the liver, completing the detachment of the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to confirm the biliary anatomy and check for any bile leaks, ensuring the surgical area is properly visualized.
The surgeon completes the robotic cholecystectomy by laparoscopically removing the gallbladder specimen using an endobag.
